currentUse: Private residential — "22nd Street Condos," 12 luxury two-story condominiums at 2132 Sarah Street, South Side Flats, Pittsburgh, PA 15203; the adjacent former St. Casimir school was converted to apartments/condos earlier (circa 2007).
summary: The 1902 church of Šv. Kazimiero (St. Casimir), Pittsburgh's first Lithuanian parish (founded 1893), still stands at 22nd and Sarah Streets in the South Side Flats and is now the "22nd Street Condos" — 12 luxury two-story condominiums of 1,200–2,300 sq ft. After the parish merged with six other South Side parishes into Prince of Peace in 1992, the pews, organ and other artifacts were shipped to Holy Trinity Church in Pilviškiai, Lithuania, and the desanctified building spent roughly a decade as a single-family residence before being sold in January 2017 for $578,517 to HHF LLC (developer Ivor Hill with partners Jim Hankle and Viktor Fischer), who carried out the condo conversion with move-ins planned from July 2018. Much of the stained glass and ornate molding was preserved, and units remain sought-after: the four-story bell-tower unit carved from the choir loft was listed at $875,000 in October 2025. The successor parish chain continued shrinking — Prince of Peace merged with St. Mary of the Mount on January 4, 2021 to form Mary, Queen of Peace Parish, which worships at St. Adalbert and St. Mary of the Mount, so no former St. Casimir building serves any religious use. The parish's St. Casimir Cemetery on Hamilton Road in Whitehall is still administered by the Diocese of Pittsburgh, remains well maintained, and still sells plots. Lithuanian communal life in the region survives outside the parish structure through the Lithuanian Citizens' Society of Western Pennsylvania (chartered 1912), active as of July 2026 with language, dance and cultural programming in Jefferson Hills.

developments.headline: Parish merged into Prince of Peace; interior shipped to Lithuania
developments.detail: St. Casimir merged with six other South Side parishes to form Prince of Peace Parish in 1992. The church's pews, organ and other artifacts were transferred to Holy Trinity Church in Pilviškiai, Lithuania. The building was desanctified; a single family later occupied part of it while the rest sat unused.

sources.title: Long-shuttered St. Casimir Church will be resurrected as 22nd St. Residences
Pittsburgh area: Lithuanians and Lithuanian heritage

developments.headline: Adjacent St. Casimir school converted to condos
developments.detail: The former parish school next door to the church was converted to condominiums in 2007; it still bears the 'St. Casimir High School' inscription over its entrance and is known as the Casimir apartments.

sources.title: Long-shuttered St. Casimir Church will be resurrected as 22nd St. Residences
Pittsburgh area: Lithuanians and Lithuanian heritage

developments.headline: Church sold for $578,517 to HHF LLC for condo conversion
developments.detail: After serving about 10 years as a single-family residence, the church was sold in January 2017 for $578,517 to developer Ivor Hill and partners Jim Hankle and Viktor Fischer under HHF LLC. Plans by Indovina Associates Architects created 12 two-story luxury condos (1,200–2,300 sq ft, priced from $350,000 to the high $500,000s), preserving much of the stained glass and ornate molding, with move-in targeted for July 2018.

sources.title: Long-shuttered St. Casimir Church will be resurrected as 22nd St. Residences
The History of St. Casimir - Pittsburgh (The Building)

developments.headline: Successor parish Prince of Peace merged into Mary, Queen of Peace
developments.detail: Under the diocese's 'On Mission for the Church Alive' restructuring, Prince of Peace (the 1992 successor to St. Casimir and six other South Side parishes) merged with St. Mary of the Mount on January 4, 2021 to form Mary, Queen of Peace Parish, which worships at the St. Adalbert and St. Mary of the Mount church buildings — ending any distinct South Side successor identity.

sources.title: Parish Histories - Mary, Queen of Peace Parish

developments.headline: Bell-tower condo in former church listed at $875,000
developments.detail: Unit 207, a four-story, 2,300 sq ft condo carved from the former choir loft with private access to a bell tower offering 360-degree city views, was listed for $875,000 — evidence the converted building remains high-end residential real estate with preserved plasterwork and arches.

sources.title: Once a Church, This Ornate South Side Condo Has Its Own Belfry Tower

developments.headline: Cemetery and regional Lithuanian society still active
developments.detail: St. Casimir Cemetery on Hamilton Road in Whitehall, the parish's former cemetery, is administered by the Diocese of Pittsburgh, remains well maintained, and still has plots available. The Lithuanian Citizens' Society of Western Pennsylvania (chartered 1912, based in Jefferson Hills) remains active with language classes, dance, singing and events updated as of July 14, 2026 — though it makes no mention of St. Casimir parish.

conflictsWithArchiveRecord: Core archive facts confirmed: closed 1992, diocesan (RC) parish, converted to 12 luxury condominiums, South Side ("Southern Pittsburgh"), not anthracite region. Two refinements: (1) The archive's "sold by the diocese to private developers" compresses the timeline — after the 1992 closure the desanctified building served roughly 10 years as a single-family residence, and the sale to the condo developers (HHF LLC — Ivor Hill, Jim Hankle, Viktor Fischer, January 2017, $578,517) came from that private ownership chain 25 years after closure, not directly from the diocese to the condo developers; the diocese's original disposition of the building (date/price/buyer) was not found in opened sources. (2) The archive lists founding as "not stated" — sources confirm the parish was founded in 1893 as Pittsburgh's first Lithuanian parish, with the surviving church building dedicated in 1902. Additionally, the 1992 ending was a merger into Prince of Peace Parish (with six other South Side parishes) rather than a bare closure, consistent with "diocese_closed."
gaps: The Pittsburgh Post-Gazette's June 14, 2019 "Buying Here" feature on the completed condos could not be read (article body paywalled/blocked), so completion-era unit sale details rely on NEXTpittsburgh and Pittsburgh Magazine. The Diocese of Pittsburgh's own parish-history page (diopitt.org/saint-casimir-south-side) is Cloudflare-blocked to automated fetching and was not read directly; sacramental-record custody was therefore not verified (presumably Mary, Queen of Peace, but unconfirmed). The identity of the private owner who held the building as a single-family residence circa 2007–2017, and the price/date of the diocese's original sale out of church ownership, were not found. No preservation campaigns, parishioner reunions, or recent press specifically about the Lithuanian community and the former church (beyond real-estate coverage) surfaced in searches. Confirmed the Lithuanian match: sources explicitly identify this St. Casimir as Pittsburgh's first Lithuanian parish; the Polish South Side parishes of the 1992 merger (St. Adalbert, St. Josaphat) are distinct.
